([/məˈskiːɡən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*mə-SKEE-gən*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a city in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Muskegon County, Michigan](/wiki/Muskegon_County,_Michigan "Muskegon County, Michigan"), United States.[[7]](#cite_note-GR6-7) Situated around a harbor of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"), Muskegon is known for fishing, sailing [regattas](/wiki/Regatta "Regatta"), and boating. It is the most populous city along Lake Michigan's eastern shore. At the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the city's population was 38,318.[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2020-3) The city is administratively autonomous from adjacent [Muskegon Township](/wiki/Muskegon_Township,_Michigan "Muskegon Township, Michigan"), and several locations in Muskegon Township and other surrounding townships have Muskegon addresses.

**Muskegon** ([/məˈskiːɡən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*mə-SKEE-gən*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a city in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Muskegon County, Michigan](/wiki/Muskegon_County,_Michigan "Muskegon County, Michigan"), United States.[[7]](#cite_note-GR6-7) Situated around a harbor of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"), Muskegon is known for fishing, sailing [regattas](/wiki/Regatta "Regatta"), and boating. It is the most populous city along Lake Michigan's eastern shore. At the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the city's population was 38,318.[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2020-3) The city is administratively autonomous from adjacent [Muskegon Township](/wiki/Muskegon_Township,_Michigan "Muskegon Township, Michigan"), and several locations in Muskegon Township and other surrounding townships have Muskegon addresses.

Muskegon is the center of the Muskegon [metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area"), which is coextensive with Muskegon County and had a population of 175,824 as of the 2020 census.[[4]](#cite_note-Metro_2020-4) It is also part of the larger [Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan")-[Kentwood](/wiki/Kentwood,_Michigan "Kentwood, Michigan")-Muskegon-[combined statistical area](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_metropolitan_area "Grand Rapids metropolitan area").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Muskegon_Michigan_harbor_entrance.jpg/250px-Muskegon_Michigan_harbor_entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muskegon_Michigan_harbor_entrance.jpg)

The entrance to [Muskegon Lake](/wiki/Muskegon_Lake "Muskegon Lake") from [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan")

The name "Muskegon" is derived from the [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa_dialect "Ottawa dialect") *mashkiigong*, meaning "marshy river or swamp".[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The "Masquigon" River ([Muskegon River](/wiki/Muskegon_River "Muskegon River")) was identified on French maps dating from the late 17th century, suggesting French explorers had reached Michigan's western coast by that time. Father [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette") traveled northward through the area on his fateful trip to [St. Ignace](/wiki/St._Ignace "St. Ignace") in 1675, and a party of French soldiers under [La Salle](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9-Robert_Cavelier,_Sieur_de_La_Salle "René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle")'s lieutenant, [Henry de Tonty](/wiki/Henry_de_Tonty "Henry de Tonty"), passed through the area in 1679.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11)

The county's earliest known Euro-American resident was Edward Fitzgerald, a [fur trader](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") and trapper who came to the Muskegon area in 1748 and who died there, reportedly being buried in the vicinity of [White Lake](/wiki/White_Lake_(Michigan) "White Lake (Michigan)"). Between 1790 and 1800, a French-Canadian trader named [Joseph La Framboise](/wiki/Magdelaine_Laframboise "Magdelaine Laframboise") established a fur-trading post at the mouth of Duck Lake. Between 1810 and 1820, several [French-Canadian](/wiki/French-Canadian "French-Canadian") fur traders, including Lamar Andie, Jean Baptiste Recollect, and Pierre Constant, had established fur-trading posts around [Muskegon Lake](/wiki/Muskegon_Lake "Muskegon Lake").[[11]](#cite_note-:0-11)

Euro-American settlement of Muskegon began in earnest in 1837, which coincided with the beginning of the exploitation of the area's extensive timber resources. The explosion of the lumber industry in 1837 inaugurated what some regard as the most romantic era in the history of the region. Lumbering in the mid-19th century brought many settlers, particularly from [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden").[[12]](#cite_note-St_Mary-12)

Some Muskegon neighborhoods began as separate villages. Bluffton was founded as a lumbering village in 1862 in [Laketon Township](/wiki/Laketon_Township,_Michigan "Laketon Township, Michigan"). It had its own post office from 1868 until 1892. Muskegon annexed it in 1889.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Muskegon is in western Muskegon County, on the south side of [Muskegon Lake](/wiki/Muskegon_Lake "Muskegon Lake"), a harbor of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). The city is bordered to the north, across Muskegon Lake, by the city of [North Muskegon](/wiki/North_Muskegon,_Michigan "North Muskegon, Michigan") and [Laketon Township](/wiki/Laketon_Township,_Michigan "Laketon Township, Michigan"). It is bordered to the east by [Muskegon Township](/wiki/Muskegon_Township,_Michigan "Muskegon Township, Michigan"), to the southeast by [Fruitport Township](/wiki/Fruitport_Township,_Michigan "Fruitport Township, Michigan"), to the south by the cities of [Muskegon Heights](/wiki/Muskegon_Heights,_Michigan "Muskegon Heights, Michigan"), [Roosevelt Park](/wiki/Roosevelt_Park,_Michigan "Roosevelt Park, Michigan"), and [Norton Shores](/wiki/Norton_Shores,_Michigan "Norton Shores, Michigan"), and to the west by Lake Michigan. It is 42 miles (68 km) northwest of [Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan"), 36 miles (58 km) north of [Holland](/wiki/Holland,_Michigan "Holland, Michigan"), and 58 miles (93 km) south of [Ludington](/wiki/Ludington,_Michigan "Ludington, Michigan").

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau"), the city has an area of 18.20 square miles (47.14 km2), of which 4.05 square miles (10.49 km2), or 22.3%, are covered by water.[[2]](#cite_note-CenPopGazetteer2023-2) The [Muskegon River](/wiki/Muskegon_River "Muskegon River") empties into Muskegon Lake at the city's northeast end.

In May 2022, local environmental groups announced cleanup efforts along Muskegon Lake officially have been completed, leading the Environmental Protection Agency to begin its study to remove Muskegon Lake from the EPA's list of "Areas of Concern", which was expected to be finalized by the end of 2022 and promised additional new economic activity in the downtown and nearby lakefront neighborhoods.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Climate")]

Muskegon has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([*Dfa*](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")) with hot summers and cold winters. Precipitation is consistent year-round. Muskegon receives heavy [lake-effect snow](/wiki/Lake-effect_snow "Lake-effect snow") from [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") during winter.

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[20]](#cite_note-wwwcensusgov-21) of 2010, 38,401 people, 13,967 households, and 7,895 families resided in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 2,702.4 inhabitants per square mile (1,043.4/km2). The 16,105 housing units had an average density of 1,133.4 per square mile (437.6/km2). The [racial makeup](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)") of the city was 57.0% White, 34.5% African American, 0.9% Native American, 0.4% Asian, 2.6% from other races, and 4.5% from two or more races. Hispanics or Latinos of any race were 8.2% of the population.

Of the 13,967 households, 32.5% had children under 18 living with them, 27.9% were married couples living together, 22.9% had a female householder with no husband present, 5.8% had a male householder with no wife present, and 43.5% were not families. About 36.0% of all households were made up of individuals, and 12.8% had someone living alone who was 65 or older. The average household size was 2.38. and the average family size was 3.09.

The median age in the city was 34.1 years; the age distribution was 23.3% were under 18; 12.2% from 18 and 24; 28.8% from 25 to 44; 24.1% from 45 to 64, and 11.6% were 65 or older. The city's gender makeup was 52.1% male and 47.9% female.

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[5]](#cite_note-GR2-5) of 2000,[[21]](#cite_note-22) 40,105 people, 14,569 households, and 8,537 families were residing in the city. The population density was 2,794.5 inhabitants per square mile (1,079.0/km2). The 15,999 housing units had an average density of 1,114.8/sq mi (430.4/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 57.9% White, 31.7% African American, 2.3% Native American, 0.46% Asian, 2.7% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 3.50% from two or more races. Hispanic or Latino people of any origins were 6.4% of the population.

Of the 14,569 households, 31.1% had children under 18 living with them, 33.2% were married couples living together, 20.2% had a female householder with no husband present, and 41.4% were not families. About 34.4% of all households were made up of individuals, and 12.9% had someone living alone who was 65 or older. The average household size was 2.42, and the average family size was 3.13.

In the city, the age distribution was 25.8% under 18, 11.6% from 18 to 24, 32.2% from 25 to 44, 18.0% from 45 to 64, and 12.4% who were 65 age or older. The median age was 32 years. For every 100 females, there were 109.6 males. For every 100 females 18 and over, there were 110.3 males.

The city's median income for a household was $27,929, and for a family was $32,640. Males had a median income of $29,114 versus $22,197 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $14,283. About 16.8% of families and 20.5% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 27.6% of those under age 18 and 14.3% of those 65 or over.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/VanDyk_Mortgage_Convention_Center_01.jpg/250px-VanDyk_Mortgage_Convention_Center_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:VanDyk_Mortgage_Convention_Center_01.jpg)

VanDyk Mortgage Convention Center

Downtown Muskegon serves as the hub for much of Muskegon County. Positioned along the southern shoreline of Muskegon Lake, it stretches for nearly two miles. Downtown is home to a number of hotels, a 25,000-square-foot convention center completed in 2021, and the [Trinity Health Arena](/wiki/Trinity_Health_Arena "Trinity Health Arena").[[22]](#cite_note-23) The Muskegon Farmer's Market welcomes more than 10,000 visitors every Saturday in the summer, and the boutique incubator shops and chalets on Western Avenue are a popular attraction for residents and tourists looking to support small businesses.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Michigan_Theater.jpg/250px-Michigan_Theater.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michigan_Theater.jpg)

[Frauenthal Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Frauenthal_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Frauenthal Center for the Performing Arts")

Muskegon is home to several historical war ships and vessels. The USS Silversides Submarine Museum[[24]](#cite_note-25) which features [USS *Silversides*](/wiki/USS_Silversides_(SS-236) "USS Silversides (SS-236)"), a World War II submarine; [USS *LST-393*](/wiki/USS_LST-393 "USS LST-393"), a World War II [tank landing ship](/wiki/Tank_landing_ship "Tank landing ship"); and USCGC *McLane*, a Prohibition-era [United States Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard") cutter. In addition, Muskegon also berths [*Milwaukee Clipper*](/wiki/SS_Milwaukee_Clipper "SS Milwaukee Clipper"), a former passenger ship built in 1904 that traveled the same route as [Lake Express](/wiki/Lake_Express "Lake Express") does today.

The [Frauenthal Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Frauenthal_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Frauenthal Center for the Performing Arts") includes two theaters, the main historic Frauenthal house and the smaller Beardsley Theater in the adjoining Hilt Building.[[25]](#cite_note-26) It was refurbished in 1998 and again in 2021, and hosts JAM Theatrical productions, Muskegon Civic Theatre productions, and is home of the [West Michigan Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/West_Michigan_Symphony_Orchestra "West Michigan Symphony Orchestra").[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28) The Frauenthal was originally built as the Michigan Theater in 1929.

The [Muskegon Museum of Art](/wiki/Muskegon_Museum_of_Art "Muskegon Museum of Art") opened in 1912. Among the highlights of its permanent collection is *Tornado Over Kansas*, by [John Steuart Curry](/wiki/John_Steuart_Curry "John Steuart Curry").[[28]](#cite_note-29) Muskegon has a growing collection of publicly owned and displayed art pieces. More than two dozen pieces are on permanent display, predominately in the downtown area.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/USS_Silversides_-_2017_%2837055099595%29.jpg/250px-USS_Silversides_-_2017_%2837055099595%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_Silversides_-_2017_(37055099595).jpg)

[USS *Silversides*](/wiki/USS_Silversides_(SS-236) "USS Silversides (SS-236)") at the USS Silversides Submarine Museum

Lakeshore Museum Center and Hackley & Hume Historic Site, built by Muskegon's [lumber barons](/wiki/Lumber_baron "Lumber baron"), are restored and open to the public.[[29]](#cite_note-30) The [Hackley](/wiki/Charles_H._Hackley_House "Charles H. Hackley House") & [Hume](/wiki/Hume_House "Hume House") mansions are part of downtown Muskegon's Heritage Village—two blocks from Muskegon Lake, and a National Register Historic District. The mansions are operated with the Lakeshore Museum Center, which details the history of Muskegon County, from the [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi") and [Odawa](/wiki/Odawa "Odawa") [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribes and lakeside fur traders to the Lumber Queen of the World to today.

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Muskegon_Breakwater_Light.jpg/250px-Muskegon_Breakwater_Light.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muskegon_Breakwater_Light.jpg)

Muskegon Break Water Light on Lake Michigan

[Pere Marquette Beach](/wiki/Pere_Marquette_Beach "Pere Marquette Beach") is the largest free public beach on the eastern shore of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). [Windsurfing](/wiki/Windsurfing "Windsurfing"), [kite boarding](/wiki/Kite_boarding "Kite boarding") competitions, and professional [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") tournaments are held there. Its quartz sand beach is expansive and bordered by large sand dunes. The beach area is popular with cyclists, runners, and hikers, and families. It faces to the west so sunsets are remarkable.

Muskegon Lakeshore Bike Trail allows for biking along the shores of Muskegon Lake to Lake Michigan, with two trails for bike paths, one on the east side of Muskegon and the other along the north side, which northerly connects to other trails, such as the [Hart-Montague Bike Trail](/wiki/Hart%E2%80%93Montague_Trail_State_Park "Hart–Montague Trail State Park"), making it possible to bike from Muskegon to [Hart, Michigan](/wiki/Hart,_Michigan "Hart, Michigan"), without ever leaving a bike trail.

[Muskegon State Park](/wiki/Muskegon_State_Park "Muskegon State Park") has a Winter Sports Complex that features [ice fishing](/wiki/Ice_fishing "Ice fishing"), [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing "Cross-country skiing"), [ice skating](/wiki/Ice_skating "Ice skating"), and a [luge](/wiki/Luge "Luge") track. P.J. [Hoffmaster State Park](/wiki/Hoffmaster_State_Park "Hoffmaster State Park") has many [sand dunes](/wiki/Sand_dune "Sand dune") as well as two [campgrounds](/wiki/Campground "Campground") and a public beach.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

[Michigan's Adventure](/wiki/Michigan%27s_Adventure "Michigan's Adventure"), the largest amusement park in the state, is in [Muskegon County](/wiki/Muskegon_County "Muskegon County"), a few miles north of the city of Muskegon. Michigan's Adventure features a midway with roller coasters, other rides, amusements, and a full water park.

[Muskegon Country Club](/wiki/Muskegon_Country_Club "Muskegon Country Club") was founded in 1908 and features a course design by [Tom Bendelow](/wiki/Tom_Bendelow "Tom Bendelow") and a course redesign by [Donald Ross](/wiki/Donald_Ross_(golfer) "Donald Ross (golfer)").

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Government")]

See also: [List of mayors of Muskegon, Michigan](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Muskegon,_Michigan "List of mayors of Muskegon, Michigan")

The city operates under a Commission-Manager form of local government. A seven-member city commission, comprising a mayor and six commissioners, serves as the legislative and executive branch. Four commissioners are elected from wards, while two commissioners are elected at large. The mayor is also elected at large and serves as chairman of the commission. The city commission hires a city manager to manage the daily operations of the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Hackley_Public_Library.jpg/250px-Hackley_Public_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hackley_Public_Library.jpg)

[Hackley Library](/wiki/Hackley_Library "Hackley Library")

[Muskegon Public Schools](/wiki/Muskegon_Public_Schools "Muskegon Public Schools") was founded in 1860 and serves students from preschool through grade 12. Additionally, it runs the Muskegon Training and Education Center. Muskegon is also served by these private K-12 schools: [Muskegon Catholic Central](/wiki/Muskegon_Catholic_Central_High_School "Muskegon Catholic Central High School"), Fruitport Calvary Christian, and [Western Michigan Christian](/wiki/Western_Michigan_Christian "Western Michigan Christian").[[34]](#cite_note-35)

Muskegon is also served by [Muskegon Community College](/wiki/Muskegon_Community_College "Muskegon Community College") and [Baker College](/wiki/Baker_College "Baker College"). [Western Michigan University](/wiki/Western_Michigan_University "Western Michigan University"), [Ferris State University](/wiki/Ferris_State_University "Ferris State University"), and [Grand Valley State University](/wiki/Grand_Valley_State_University "Grand Valley State University") all operate programs out of the [Stevenson Center for Higher Education](/w/index.php?title=Stevenson_Center_for_Higher_Education&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stevenson Center for Higher Education (page does not exist)") on the [Muskegon Community College](/wiki/Muskegon_Community_College "Muskegon Community College") campus.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Media")]

Muskegon's leading newspaper is *The [Muskegon Chronicle](/wiki/Muskegon_Chronicle "Muskegon Chronicle")*. *The Chronicle* is a daily newspaper owned by [Booth Newspapers](/wiki/Booth_Newspapers "Booth Newspapers"). It started publication in 1857.

Muskegon is served by the local television station [WMKG-CD](/wiki/WMKG-CD "WMKG-CD").

The Muskegon area is also served by several radio stations. [WUVS-LP](/wiki/WUVS-LP "WUVS-LP") 103.7 is a popular urban (hip-hop/R&B) and gospel station with local programming as well as Sunday religious programming and local-based talk. Another local low-powered FM station is [WUGM-LP](/wiki/WUGM-LP "WUGM-LP") 106.1, owned by the Muskegon Training and Education Center, which airs an [Urban Oldies](/wiki/Urban_adult_contemporary "Urban adult contemporary") format dubbed "M-TEC 106 FM, Rock 'n' Soul." A Newer LP-FM Station [WFFR-LP](/wiki/WFFR-LP "WFFR-LP") 100.9 also offers local programming along with a classic hits format. The station is based out of nearby Roosevelt Park. Other local FM stations include 90.3 [WBLV](/wiki/WBLV "WBLV")-FM (classical/jazz/NPR), 91.7 [WMCQ](/wiki/WMCQ "WMCQ")-FM (religious), [WWSN](/wiki/WWSN_(FM) "WWSN (FM)") FM 92.5 (adult contemporary), [WGVS-FM](/wiki/WGVS-FM "WGVS-FM") 95.3 (public radio), [WLAW-FM](/wiki/WLAW-FM "WLAW-FM") FM 97.5 (country), [WLCS](/wiki/WLCS "WLCS")-FM 98.3 (oldies), [WVIB](/wiki/WVIB "WVIB")-FM 100.1 (urban contemporary), [WMRR](/wiki/WMRR "WMRR")-FM 101.7 (classic rock), [WSNX](/wiki/WSNX "WSNX")-FM 104.5 (top 40, studios in Grand Rapids), [WOOD-FM](/wiki/WOOD-FM "WOOD-FM") 106.9 (news/talk, simulcast of WOOD-AM 1300/Grand Rapids), and [WMUS](/wiki/WMUS "WMUS") FM 107.9 (country). Other local AM stations aside from WKBZ include [WSMZ](/wiki/WSMZ_(AM) "WSMZ (AM)") 850, [WLAW](/wiki/WLAW_(AM) "WLAW (AM)") 1490 (country).

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/ISS067-E-174325_Muskegon%2C_Michigan.jpg/250px-ISS067-E-174325_Muskegon%2C_Michigan.jpg)](/wiki/File:ISS067-E-174325_Muskegon,_Michigan.jpg)

Muskegon as seen from the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station") in 2022

Public transportation is provided by the [Muskegon Area Transit System](/wiki/Muskegon_Area_Transit_System "Muskegon Area Transit System") (MATS – "The Shore Line"), which operates nine bus routes, three trolley routes, and a paratransit system.[[35]](#cite_note-36) MATS serves the Herman Ivory Passenger Terminal.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

MATS operates the Muskegon Trolley Company. Three routes cover the north side, south side, and downtown; each trolley stops at 11 locations, including Hackley and Hume Historic Site, USS Silversides, and Muskegon State Park.

Greyhound Lines operated out of downtown Muskegon for many years as the end point of their cross-state route along I-96 to Detroit. Services were suspended during the severe downturn in travel during the COVID Pandemic of 2020. While regional bus carrier Indian Trails picked up the bulk of Greyhound’s former Michigan services, no intercity coach service has been restored to Muskegon as of 2025.

Commercial air service is currently provided by [Denver Air Connection](/wiki/Denver_Air_Connection "Denver Air Connection") operating regional jet flights at [Muskegon County Airport](/wiki/Muskegon_County_Airport "Muskegon County Airport") (MKG), with nonstop service to [Chicago O'Hare Airport](/wiki/Chicago_O%27Hare_Airport "Chicago O'Hare Airport"). Other airlines provide passenger service via the [Gerald R. Ford International Airport](/wiki/Gerald_R._Ford_International_Airport "Gerald R. Ford International Airport") (GRR) in [Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan").

Muskegon is the eastern port of the [Lake Express](/wiki/Lake_Express "Lake Express") High-Speed Car Ferry that crosses Lake Michigan to [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") offering three roundtrips a day in the summer, and two roundtrips in the fall.

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Union_Depot_-_Muskegon_01.jpg/250px-Union_Depot_-_Muskegon_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Union_Depot_-_Muskegon_01.jpg)

[Union Depot](/wiki/Muskegon_station "Muskegon station")

The Michigan Shore Railway, provides freight rail service for many of Muskegon's industries. Rail passenger services ended in 1971 with the advent of Amtrak; the Chesapeake and Ohio Railroad’s routes in Michigan were not included in the initial Amtrak system. The nearest passenger rail available is via [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") in nearby [Holland](/wiki/Holland,_Michigan "Holland, Michigan") or Grand Rapids. Until 1971, the [Chesapeake & Ohio Railway](/wiki/Chesapeake_%26_Ohio_Railway "Chesapeake & Ohio Railway") (successor to the [Pere Marquette Railway](/wiki/Pere_Marquette_Railway "Pere Marquette Railway")) operated day and night trains from [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Depot_(Muskegon,_Michigan) "Union Depot (Muskegon, Michigan)") to [Holland](/wiki/Holland,_Michigan "Holland, Michigan") and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"). The C&O became a unit of [Chessie System](/wiki/Chessie_System "Chessie System") in 1973 and subsequently [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation") in 1987. CSX leased their Muskegon area lines to MS in 2005.[[37]](#cite_note-38) The [Grand Trunk Western](/wiki/Grand_Trunk_Western "Grand Trunk Western") and the [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad") had earlier operated passenger trains out of another Muskegon station to various points in Michigan.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

### Ferries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muskegon,_Michigan&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Ferries")]

In 1937, the Grand Trunk Western began operating ferries that met up with train and carried passengers and automobiles across Lake Michigan to Milwaukee. Earlier, the GTW had operated the ferries out of [Grand Haven](/wiki/Grand_Haven,_Michigan "Grand Haven, Michigan"). The GTW stopped operating the ferries in 1978. The last remaining ferries across the lake would be the ones launching from [Ludington, Michigan](/wiki/Ludington,_Michigan "Ludington, Michigan")[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41) until the [Lake Express](/wiki/HSC_Lake_Express "HSC Lake Express") first came into service on June 1, 2004.[[41]](#cite_note-42)
